refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality/reliability obligation in the problem domain without prescribing how (e.g., protocol selection, redundancy, error correction). The phrase 'customer-defined' remains because the acceptance threshold is a stakeholder-negotiated constraint, not a design choice. This formulation is solution-neutral and atomic.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profile, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss across representative mission durations and worksite locations. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions and acceptance sign-off by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback transmission with packet loss ≤ [customer-defined limit] under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and network condition feedback
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover to maintain continuous connectivity across independent network routes
DP3: Deploy quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to reserve bandwidth for critical teleoperation commands and reduce contention-induced loss
DP4: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to network conditions and loss thresholds
DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission for critical commands, FEC for continuous streams, and QoS prioritization across a monitored network path
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission for critical commands, FEC for continuous streams, and QoS prioritization across a monitored network path

potential_sources: Coupling between retransmission logic and FEC encoder state management—mitigated by clear separation: retransmission handles command-level semantics; FEC handles frame-level redundancy
Coupling between QoS prioritization and packet classification—managed by defining a priority tagging scheme independent of specific network hardware
Coupling between loss detection and adaptation algorithms—risk of feedback loops; mitigated by defining stable thresholds and hysteresis bands to prevent oscillation
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids tight coupling to specific network technologies (e.g., 5G, WiFi, satellite) by defining logical control loops and modular encoding/retransmission strategies. Each sub-mechanism (retransmission, FEC, QoS) can be independently tuned and disabled if network conditions permit. The design assumes a monitoring layer that observes loss rates and triggers adaptation—this is a clear dependency that must be realized as a separate IM.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network path supports in-band or out-of-band loss measurement (e.g., ACK feedback, probe packets). RISK: If measurement is unavailable or delayed, adaptation will be reactive and may overshoot or undershoot the target loss criterion.

ASSUMPTION: Retransmission latency budget exists (i.e., teleoperation commands tolerate round-trip delay for retry). RISK: If latency is critical (e.g., <100 ms), aggressive retransmission may violate safety-critical timing; FEC becomes the primary mechanism, reducing adaptability.

ASSUMPTION: FEC overhead (redundancy ratio) can be dynamically adjusted without destabilizing the control loop. RISK: Excessive FEC overhead may saturate the network, paradoxically increasing loss; tuning requires empirical validation.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is achievable under agreed network conditions. RISK: If baseline loss is >1% due to infrastructure limitations, no DP can meet the FR without network upgrade or relaxed acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands and sensor streams have distinct loss tolerance profiles (commands more critical than continuous telemetry). RISK: If all traffic is equally critical, selective prioritization may not be justified; uniform FEC may be more cost-effective.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits temporary communication degradation during adaptation transients. RISK: If safety interlocks require zero-loss guarantees, DP5 must include a safe-state fallback (e.g., operator-initiated pause) that is not addressed in this DP alone.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability such that packet loss remains ≤ [customer-defined target, e.g., 1%] under agreed network conditions, with explicit definition of: (1) which traffic classes (commands vs. telemetry) are subject to the criterion; (2) acceptable latency budget for retransmission; (3) safe-state behavior if the criterion is violated; (4) network measurement and feedback mechanism (in-band or out-of-band). DP5 shall be decomposed into three child FRs: FR5a (Detect and measure packet loss in real time), FR5b (Selectively retransmit critical commands within latency budget), FR5c (Apply FEC and QoS prioritization to reduce loss below criterion). Each child FR shall map to a distinct IM (loss monitor module, command retransmission handler, FEC/QoS policy engine) to enable independent testing and tuning.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all network communication logic, QoS management, retransmission strategies, and forward error correction for teleoperated command and telemetry streams

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation module applies selective retransmission and QoS prioritization to critical tasking payloads

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle state and sensor data; Teleoperation module applies FEC to continuous telemetry streams and monitors network path quality

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to physical/logical network; Teleoperation module manages QoS parameters, monitors latency/loss, and implements hybrid retransmission + FEC strategy

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates network degradation events and QoS violations to Safety Services when thresholds are breached

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of command/telemetry transport, QoS prioritization, and retransmission logic. Selective retransmission applies directly to critical tasking commands; FEC applies to continuous telemetry streams. Network path monitoring is intrinsic to teleoperation. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert escalation requires coupling to Safety Services, but this is a thin, well-defined interface.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics and actuators, not network transport. Placing hybrid retransmission + FEC + QoS logic in Vehicle creates high coupling to network infrastructure and violates separation of concerns. Would require Vehicle to manage network state, which is not its responsibility.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety policy and monitoring, not network transport mechanics. While QoS violations may trigger safety escalations, the implementation of retransmission, FEC, and QoS prioritization is not a safety function—it is a communication function. Placing DP5 here conflates safety policy with transport implementation.

Moderate fit but overly broad: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. While it could logically contain Teleoperation, assigning DP5 directly to NGHE Solution bypasses the more specific Teleoperation boundary and reduces architectural clarity. Prefer the narrower, more cohesive Teleoperation module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP5. It owns the end-to-end command and telemetry transport pipeline, making it the natural home for selective retransmission (critical commands), FEC (continuous streams), and QoS prioritization. Network path monitoring is intrinsic to teleoperation quality assurance. The module interfaces cleanly with Tasking (inbound commands), Telemetry (outbound state), Network Link (transport substrate), and SafetyAlert (escalation on QoS breach). Containment is strong; diffusion is minimal and well-managed.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module independently implements its own retransmission or error correction for actuator commands, DP5 logic may diffuse across module boundaries. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership: Teleoperation owns all network-level transport; Vehicle consumes commands via Tasking interface and trusts Teleoperation's delivery guarantees.

Risk: If Safety Services implements its own QoS monitoring or network health checks, duplication and inconsistency may arise. Mitigation: Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert notifications from Teleoperation; it does not independently monitor network state.

Risk: If multiple subsystems (e.g., Vehicle, Safety Services) each define their own retransmission or FEC policies, the hybrid strategy becomes fragmented. Mitigation: Teleoperation is the single point of policy definition and enforcement for all network transport.

Risk: Network Link interface may expose low-level transport details that tempt other modules to bypass Teleoperation. Mitigation: Enforce architectural layering: only Teleoperation directly manages Network Link; other modules interact via Tasking/Telemetry.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid selective retransmission + FEC + QoS prioritization) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication reliability within packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%). The DP is solution-complete: selective retransmission targets critical commands (low latency, high reliability); FEC targets continuous streams (telemetry); QoS prioritization ensures critical payloads are serviced first. The monitored network path enables adaptive strategy tuning. No logical gaps between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Vehicle module may independently implement command validation or retry logic for actuator commands, creating duplicate retransmission semantics. Mitigation: Establish clear contract—Vehicle trusts Teleoperation delivery guarantees; Vehicle does not retry or validate transport-level reliability.

Risk: Safety Services may implement its own network health monitoring or QoS thresholds, leading to inconsistent escalation policies. Mitigation: Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert notifications from Teleoperation as the single source of truth for network degradation events.

Risk: Multiple subsystems may attempt direct access to Network Link interface to implement custom error handling, bypassing Teleoperation's unified strategy. Mitigation: Enforce strict layering—only Teleoperation manages Network Link; all other modules interact via Tasking/Telemetry abstractions.

Risk: FEC codec selection and retransmission timeout tuning may be scattered across configuration files, policy documents, and code, making the hybrid strategy difficult to audit and update. Mitigation: Centralize all DP5 parameters (FEC redundancy ratio, retransmission window, QoS class mappings) in a single Teleoperation configuration module with clear ownership and versioning.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Excellent fit: module owns end-to-end command/telemetry transport, QoS management, and network path monitoring—all core to DP5. Interfaces are clean and well-defined: Tasking (inbound), Telemetry (outbound), Network Link (substrate), SafetyAlert (escalation). Minor diffusion (SafetyAlert coupling) is acceptable and thin. No alternative module (Vehicle, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) provides better cohesion or lower coupling. Recommend Teleoperation as the canonical IM boundary for DP5.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is measurable and testable. Teleoperation module can expose metrics (observed packet loss %, FEC overhead, retransmission rate, QoS class distribution) via instrumentation. Test scenarios can simulate network degradation (packet loss injection, latency spikes, jitter) and verify that DP5 strategies maintain observed loss ≤ acceptance threshold. SafetyAlert escalation can be verified by injecting QoS violations and confirming Safety Services receives notifications. Network path monitoring can be validated by observing latency/loss metrics under controlled conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to DP5, which is owned by Teleoperation IM. Teleoperation's scope explicitly includes 'network communication logic, QoS management, retransmission strategies, and forward error correction for teleoperated command and telemetry streams.' This directly maps to DP5 implementation. Tasking and Telemetry interfaces define the contract boundaries; Network Link interface defines the transport substrate. SafetyAlert interface defines the escalation path. No ambiguity in responsibility assignment.

recommended_revisions: Establish a formal interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle: Vehicle shall not implement transport-level retransmission or error correction; it shall consume commands via Tasking interface and assume Teleoperation guarantees delivery within acceptable latency bounds.

Establish a formal interface contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services: Safety Services shall not independently monitor network health; it shall consume QoS violation events via SafetyAlert and escalate per safety policy. Teleoperation is the single source of truth for network state.

Centralize DP5 configuration (FEC codec, redundancy ratio, retransmission timeout, QoS class mappings, packet loss threshold) in a dedicated Teleoperation configuration artifact with version control and change tracking. Document the rationale for each parameter choice relative to the acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss).

Add explicit test acceptance criteria to Teleoperation module specification: 'Under simulated network conditions (X% baseline loss, Y ms latency, Z ms jitter), DP5 shall maintain observed packet loss ≤ 1% with FEC overhead ≤ [target]% and retransmission latency ≤ [target] ms.' Link to worksite safety plan and operational policies to ensure network conditions are representative.

Document the SafetyAlert escalation policy: define QoS thresholds (e.g., sustained loss >2%, latency >500ms) that trigger escalation, and confirm alignment with safety plan. Ensure Safety Services has clear guidance on remediation actions (e.g., pause mission, switch network, alert operator).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. DP5 is solution-complete and Teleoperation IM is well-scoped with acceptable diffusion (0.08). However, safety criticality is HIGH, and the recommended revisions reveal that critical interface contracts and configuration governance are not yet formalized. Rather than stopping at this level, decomposition must continue to ensure that: (1) the formal interface contracts between Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services are explicitly captured as sub-FRs with corresponding DPs and IMs; (2) the centralized configuration and parameter governance for DP5 is architecturally realized; (3) the SafetyAlert escalation policy and test acceptance criteria are formally specified and traceable. These are not gaps in DP5 logic itself, but gaps in the architectural realization and governance that must be resolved before implementation. Decomposing to the next level will make these dependencies explicit and assignable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Establish and enforce a formal interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules such that Vehicle does not implement transport-level retransmission, error correction, or command validation, and instead trusts Teleoperation delivery guarantees within specified latency bounds.

FR5.2: Establish and enforce a formal interface contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services modules such that Safety Services does not independently monitor network health, and instead consumes QoS violation events via SafetyAlert as the single source of truth for network state.

FR5.3: Centralize and version-control all DP5 configuration parameters (FEC codec, redundancy ratio, retransmission timeout, QoS class mappings, packet loss threshold) in a dedicated Teleoperation configuration artifact with documented rationale for each parameter relative to the ≤1% packet loss acceptance criterion.

FR5.4: Define and formalize the SafetyAlert escalation policy, including QoS thresholds (e.g., sustained loss >2%, latency >500ms) that trigger escalation, and align with safety plan and operator remediation procedures.

FR5.5: Specify explicit test acceptance criteria for DP5 under representative simulated network conditions (baseline loss, latency, jitter), including target bounds for observed packet loss (≤1%), FEC overhead, and retransmission latency, with traceability to worksite safety plan.

revise.why: No revisions to current FR, DP, or IM are required. All three gates pass. The recommended revisions are not corrections to DP5 logic but rather formalization and governance tasks that should be captured as sub-level FRs to ensure architectural completeness and safety compliance.

assumptions: Teleoperation module has sufficient computational and memory capacity to implement hybrid selective retransmission + FEC + QoS prioritization without introducing unacceptable latency overhead.
Network path monitoring (latency, loss, jitter) is observable and measurable via the Network Link interface with sufficient fidelity to drive adaptive strategy tuning.
Vehicle and Safety Services modules will respect formal interface contracts and not implement redundant transport-level reliability logic.
SafetyAlert notification mechanism is reliable and timely enough to enable Safety Services to escalate network degradation events before mission-critical failures occur.
FEC codec and retransmission parameters can be tuned offline (via configuration) without requiring runtime algorithm changes.
Worksite network conditions (baseline loss, latency, jitter) are known or can be characterized during commissioning to validate DP5 parameter choices.
open_risks: Risk: Interface contracts between Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services are not yet formalized. Without explicit contracts, Vehicle or Safety Services may independently implement retry logic or network monitoring, creating semantic duplication and unpredictable behavior. Mitigation: Decompose to FR5.1 and FR5.2 to formalize these contracts as architectural requirements.

Risk: DP5 configuration parameters (FEC redundancy, retransmission timeout, QoS mappings) may be scattered across code, configuration files, and documentation, making the strategy difficult to audit, update, and version-control. Mitigation: Decompose to FR5.3 to establish centralized configuration governance.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation policy is not yet formally defined. Without explicit thresholds and remediation procedures, Safety Services may not respond appropriately to network degradation. Mitigation: Decompose to FR5.4 to formalize escalation policy and align with safety plan.

Risk: Test acceptance criteria for DP5 are not yet specified. Without explicit bounds on packet loss, FEC overhead, and retransmission latency under representative network conditions, verification may be incomplete or non-reproducible. Mitigation: Decompose to FR5.5 to specify test acceptance criteria with traceability to safety plan.

Risk: High safety criticality requires that all DP5 parameters and escalation policies be auditable and traceable. Current state does not guarantee this. Mitigation: Ensure FR5.3, FR5.4, and FR5.5 are realized with full documentation and version control.
evidence_needed: Formal interface specification document (contract) between Teleoperation and Vehicle, signed off by both module owners, defining delivery guarantees, latency bounds, and prohibition on redundant retry logic.
Formal interface specification document (contract) between Teleoperation and Safety Services, defining SafetyAlert notification semantics, QoS threshold definitions, and escalation responsibilities.

Centralized Teleoperation configuration artifact (e.g., YAML, JSON, or configuration class) containing all DP5 parameters with documented rationale for each choice relative to ≤1% packet loss criterion.
SafetyAlert escalation policy document defining QoS thresholds, escalation triggers, and operator remediation procedures, with alignment to worksite safety plan.

Test plan for DP5 with explicit acceptance criteria (packet loss ≤1%, FEC overhead ≤[target]%, retransmission latency ≤[target]ms) under representative simulated network conditions, with traceability to safety plan.
Network characterization data (baseline loss, latency, jitter) from worksite or representative test environment, used to validate DP5 parameter choices.
Verification report confirming that DP5 strategies maintain observed packet loss ≤1% under test conditions, with FEC overhead and retransmission latency within acceptable bounds.
